statusLine: WTI crude trades at elevated $104.54/bbl amid heightened geopolitical risks, while US inventories draw down across all major petroleum products.
fullAnalysis:
US crude markets remain under pressure with WTI at $104.54/bbl, down just 1.07% despite significant geopolitical volatility. The unusually wide $14.16 WTI-Brent spread indicates Americas crude is commanding a premium over international benchmarks, likely reflecting supply chain disruptions and regional security concerns. US production holds steady at 13.596 million bpd, maintaining America's position as the world's largest producer, though elevated prices suggest market tightness.

Inventory drawdowns across all major petroleum categories signal robust demand and potential supply constraints. Commercial crude stocks fell 4.144 million barrels to 409.181 million barrels, while gasoline inventories dropped a substantial 6.328 million barrels to 232.944 million barrels. Distillate stocks also declined 3.122 million barrels to 111.559 million barrels. The Strategic Petroleum Reserve remains at 77.897 million barrels, well below historical norms.

Critical maritime chokepoints face severe disruption risks that could impact Americas energy security. Multiple MARAD advisories warn of Iranian attacks in the Strait of Hormuz and Persian Gulf, while Houthi forces continue targeting vessels in the Red Sea and Bab el Mandeb Strait. These disruptions force longer shipping routes and higher freight costs, contributing to the elevated WTI premium. The Panama Canal remains operational but global shipping disruptions affect tanker availability and routing flexibility.

Retail fuel prices reflect the upstream pressures, with gasoline holding steady at $4.254/gallon while diesel eased slightly to $5.608/gallon. The stability in gasoline prices despite inventory draws suggests refiners are maintaining adequate throughput, though margins likely remain compressed. Recent research indicates global fossil power generation has declined following Hormuz disruptions, partly offset by renewable energy growth, while Russian export sanctions continue reshaping global oil flows away from traditional routes.
keyPoints: WTI crude at $104.54/bbl commands $14.16 premium over Brent, indicating Americas supply tightness
US petroleum inventories decline across all categories: crude (-4.1MB), gasoline (-6.3MB), distillates (-3.1MB)
Multiple critical maritime advisories warn of Iranian attacks in Strait of Hormuz and Houthi disruptions in Red Sea
US production steady at 13.596 million bpd maintaining global leadership despite elevated prices
Retail gasoline stable at $4.254/gal while diesel drops to $5.608/gal amid inventory pressures
Strategic Petroleum Reserve at 77.9 million barrels remains well below historical averages
